baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 52.8 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1973 from Energy Institute/OWID oil consumption converted at 620 TWh per 1 mb/d-year; total-liquids denominator would be somewhat higher.
route_substitution_constraint: Embargo/allocation shock rather than a physical chokepoint closure; substitution depended on non-OAPEC supply and reallocation, not route bypass.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Low practical buffer for targeted importers; U.S. spare capacity had diminished and OAPEC spare barrels were politically withheld.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No IEA collective stockdraw system yet; response relied on allocation controls, speed limits, conservation, and later creation of emergency-stock institutions.
caveats: Price change is World Bank monthly crude-oil average deflated with FRED CPI, peak during event window vs prior-3-month average; pre-1980 benchmark is less comparable than Brent/WTI era. Denominator is oil-consumption equivalent, not a fully harmonized total-liquids balance.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 60.0 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1979 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; acute Iranian output loss occurred before full 1979-80 price shock peak.
route_substitution_constraint: Producer-regime-collapse outage; no route closure, but Iranian crude quality and contract replacement constraints mattered.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Partial offset from other producers, especially Saudi/OPEC, but tight demand, precautionary stockbuilding, and quality/location constraints limited effective buffer.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA existed but no comparable collective emergency release; private and government stockbuilding amplified the market squeeze rather than buffering it cleanly.
caveats: Price column covers Nov. 1978-Apr. 1979 acute window; Fed History notes prices more than doubled from Apr. 1979 to Apr. 1980, so this row understates the full second-oil-shock price episode.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 57.4 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1980 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; global demand was already weakening after the 1979 shock.
route_substitution_constraint: Regional war damaged export infrastructure and raised Gulf shipping risk; Strait did not fully close, so substitution was supply/terminal replacement more than route bypass.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Some OPEC replacement supply was available, but the outage hit two Gulf producers soon after the Iranian Revolution and usable spare capacity was politically and logistically constrained.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA emergency system existed but no clear collective stock release for this episode in the source set; high prices and recession/demand destruction became a major adjustment channel.
caveats: Measured price rise is small because oil prices were already extremely elevated before the war outbreak. Treat as incremental shock on top of the 1978-80 regime shift.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 61.1 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1990 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; EIA says nearly all Kuwaiti and Iraqi output went offline immediately.
route_substitution_constraint: Country-export outage and war-risk shock, not a Hormuz closure; replacement barrels from other OPEC members were the key substitution path.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Meaningful offset from Saudi/OPEC spare capacity and later restoration of market confidence; buffer was stronger than in the 1970s.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA first collective action/contingency plan in Jan. 1991; emergency measures and stockdraws helped offset the loss, with institutional sources citing roughly 2.5 mb/d equivalent planned response.
caveats: End date uses start of Operation Desert Storm as acute pre-response window; Kuwaiti infrastructure recovery continued long after the price spike.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 70.5 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 2002 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; Venezuelan production fell from roughly 3.4 mb/d before the strike to below 1 mb/d at the trough in institutional accounts.
route_substitution_constraint: Not a route shock; substitution constrained by crude quality and refinery configuration rather than maritime chokepoint capacity.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Moderate global buffer, but heavy/sour crude quality and U.S. Gulf Coast refinery fit reduced substitutability; shock overlapped with Iraq-war and Nigeria risks.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No IEA collective release identified for this strike; adjustment came through replacement crude, inventories, and demand/price response.
caveats: Attribution is messy because the strike coincided with Iraq-war risk and other disruptions; price change should not be read as Venezuela-only impact.

commodity_scope: crude oil, refined products, and refining/logistics
baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 75.8 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 2005 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; U.S. Gulf offshore output around 1.5 mb/d was shut in at peak and refining/product logistics were also disrupted.
unit: mb/d gross peak crude supply loss; refining outages larger but not included in share
route_substitution_constraint: Domestic infrastructure/refining shock; route substitution through imports was possible but constrained by product specs, port/refinery outages, and delivery timing.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Crude supply buffer was less binding than refining/product logistics; product imports, refinery recovery, and fuel waivers mattered more than upstream spare capacity alone.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA coordinated 60 million barrel release at 2 mb/d for 30 days; U.S. DOE SPR sale awarded about 11 million barrels after Katrina.
caveats: A crude-only disrupted share understates the shock because refinery capacity, pipelines, ports, and refined-product supply were central.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 78.4 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 2011 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; IEA estimated 132 million barrels of light sweet crude removed by end-May and expected disruption to persist.
route_substitution_constraint: Not a route closure; substitution constrained by crude grade, refinery configuration, and Mediterranean sourcing patterns.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Headline spare capacity existed, especially Saudi, but quality mismatch mattered because Libyan light sweet crude fit specific European refineries.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA made 60 million barrels available over one month to offset Libyan disruption while waiting for other supply to reach market.
caveats: Small global share had outsized market relevance because crude quality and refinery fit mattered; duration field uses broad civil-war disruption window, not only Feb-May cumulative loss window.

commodity_scope: crude oil processing and production capacity
baseline_or_market_size: Global petroleum-liquids market around 100 mb/d in 2019; EIA/CRS report a temporary 5.7 mb/d Saudi outage, roughly 5% of global supply.
route_substitution_constraint: Infrastructure-processing shock rather than route shock; route substitution was not binding, but facility concentration created single-point risk.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Saudi inventories, rapid repairs, and available capacity elsewhere buffered the market; however spare capacity was partly inside the affected Saudi system.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No IEA collective release; Saudi commercial/strategic storage and fast restoration were the main buffer.
caveats: Monthly average price metric masks the intraday/daily spike and fast reversal after repair assurances; use daily Brent for market-reaction charts.

case_name: 2022 Russia invasion, sanctions, and buyer strike
baseline_or_market_size: Global petroleum-liquids market near 99-100 mb/d in 2022; IEA March 2022 warned 3 mb/d of Russian oil output could be shut in from April, while pre-shock Russian exports were about 5 mb/d crude and 2.85 mb/d products.
unit: mb/d potential shut-in / at-risk output estimate
route_substitution_constraint: High substitution friction: sanctions, self-sanctioning, insurance, tanker availability, longer voyages, and destination switching turned this into a rerouting/regime-change shock rather than a clean physical outage.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Spare capacity was concentrated in a few OPEC+ producers and constrained by politics, sanctions, quality, refinery fit, shipping, and insurance; U.S. shale response was slower than acute window.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA 2022 collective actions ultimately totaled 182.7 million barrels; U.S. SPR releases were also material, but stockdraw timing differed from physical Russian rerouting.
caveats: Use 3 mb/d as IEA risk estimate, not confirmed sustained physical loss. Russia redirected large volumes, so exposed flow, realized shut-in, and rerouted flow must remain separate in later ranking.

baseline_or_market_size: IEA/OECD emergency-response histories identify the 1956-57 Suez crisis as the first major postwar oil disruption; OECD notes the crisis effectively blocked about half of canal oil transit and IEA tables give a gross peak supply loss around 2.0 mb/d.
route_substitution_constraint: Cape of Good Hope rerouting and Western Hemisphere supply substitution were available but slow and capacity-constrained for Europe; no SUMED-style pipeline bypass existed in this period.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Not quantified in this slice; use qualitative "weeks-scale" rerouting burden until an archival freight/voyage source is collected.
price_or_cost_channel: Freight, insurance, and rationing channels were material but no public institutional daily series has been collected.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. High confidence for closure dates and IEA peak-loss order of magnitude; lower confidence for daily route-flow denominator. Start date is the Tripartite Aggression date from SCA chronology; SCA says navigation resumed 1957-03-29 after clearing sunk ships. The 2.0 mb/d figure is a gross peak oil-supply disruption, not a direct daily canal transit count.

baseline_or_market_size: SCA chronology says navigation stopped after the 1967 war until international navigation resumed on 1975-06-05; IEA emergency tables identify an acute June-August 1967 oil disruption with gross peak supply loss around 2.0 mb/d.
route_substitution_constraint: Long closure forced structural adaptation: Cape of Good Hope routing, larger tankers, and altered Europe-Asia/Middle East oil logistics. Route substitution was physically feasible but required much longer voyages and fleet/terminal adaptation.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: Not quantified here; modern IEA Suez factsheet says Cape rerouting can add roughly two weeks for a prolonged Suez blockage, but that modern estimate should not be applied mechanically to 1967-75.
price_or_cost_channel: Freight-rate and tanker-fleet composition effects likely dominated after the acute shock, but public institutional series still need collection.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. High confidence for closure dates; medium confidence for translating the acute 1967 oil disruption into a long-route logistics metric. The canal was closed for nearly eight years, but the IEA 2.0 mb/d number is for the acute June-August 1967 oil-supply disruption; do not multiply 2.0 mb/d by the full closure duration.

case_name: Tanker War Persian Gulf shipping attacks
commodity_scope: oil tanker traffic and war-risk logistics
baseline_or_market_size: Strauss Center synthesis: 239 petroleum tankers attacked and 55 sunk or constructive total loss; oil tankers were 61% of attacked ships. It also reports an initial 25% drop in commercial shipping but says the Tanker War failed to disrupt more than 2% of ships passing through the Persian Gulf at the most intense point.
disrupted_share_pct: <2 of ships passing through Persian Gulf at peak according to Strauss Center
route_substitution_constraint: Traffic continued under fire with naval escorts, reflagging, insurance premia, and discounts rather than a full Strait closure; the key comparison is resilience under attack, not route elimination.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: No alternative global route avoids Hormuz for Gulf exports; mitigation was convoy/security and pricing rather than rerouting. Delay/cost not quantified in this public slice.
price_or_cost_channel: War-risk insurance and security costs rose; Strauss reports Iran lowered oil prices to offset higher insurance premiums. No consistent public premium series collected.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. Start date is approximate escalation year from the local case list and Strauss narrative; attacks began earlier in the Iran-Iraq War. Attack counts are not daily transit denominators and should not be summed with oil-supply-loss cases.

commodity_scope: containerized trade, oil, LNG, LPG, agricultural commodities
baseline_or_market_size: USDA/FAS using SCA annual data reports 18,829 vessels and 1.17 billion net tons transited Suez in 2020; about 12-13% of global trade passes through Suez annually. IEA modern factsheet says Jan-Oct 2023 Suez carried 7.5 mb/d oil and 36 bcm LNG, equal to 10% of global seaborne oil and 8% of global LNG trade.
disrupted_share_pct: 12-13 global trade exposed; 100 canal flow stopped during blockage
route_substitution_constraint: No in-canal substitute while the vessel blocked the lane; ships could wait or divert around the Cape of Good Hope. Energy flows also had partial pipeline/substitution options depending on direction and commodity.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: USDA/FAS says an Arabian Gulf-Europe oil tanker avoiding Suez would travel an additional 6000 miles and add about $300000 in energy costs; IEA says prolonged Suez blockage can add roughly two weeks around Africa.
price_or_cost_channel: USDA/FAS reports SCA lost $12-14 million revenue per day and $84-98 million over the week; Moody's estimate cited by USDA/FAS put temporary global container throughput impact at 10-15%.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. Duration is calendar-inclusive. This was mainly delay/backlog, not destroyed cargo or sustained oil supply removal. Price response is left blank because a clean real commodity-price peak attributable to the blockage was not collected.

commodity_scope: container shipping, port congestion, general maritime trade
baseline_or_market_size: UNCTAD Review of Maritime Transport 2021 says maritime trade contracted 3.8% in 2020 and then rebounded asymmetrically; RMT 2022 says 2021 maritime trade rose 3.2% to 11 billion tons but remained hampered by unprecedented logistics logjams.
route_substitution_constraint: No single route substitution constraint; the binding constraints were container availability, port labor, inland transport, blank sailings, congestion, and schedule reliability across the network.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: UNCTAD RMT 2022 says Far East-North America container schedule delays rose from 2 days in 2020Q1 to 12 days in 2021Q4; median container-ship turnaround time rose 13.7% in 2021.
price_or_cost_channel: UNCTAD says COVID-related capacity shortages and demand rebound pushed container freight rates to records; RMT 2022 says mid-2021 rates peaked at four times pre-pandemic levels and gives China-South America rates rising from about $2000/TEU in 2019 to $10196/TEU by Dec. 2021.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. Date window is an analytical approximation covering the main maritime logistics disruption, not a formal event window. This row uses freight/congestion metrics because there is no single chokepoint flow loss; leave exposed flow and disrupted share blank rather than inventing them.

case_name: Red Sea and Bab el-Mandeb shipping disruption
commodity_scope: container shipping, tankers, Suez/Bab el-Mandeb trade
baseline_or_market_size: IMF PortWatch blog says about 15% of global maritime trade volume normally passes through Suez; in Jan-Feb 2024 Suez Canal trade volume dropped 50% year over year and Cape of Good Hope trade volume rose 74%. World Bank March 2024 note says traffic through Suez and Bab el-Mandeb had halved while Cape navigation doubled.
disrupted_share_pct: 50 Suez trade volume drop y/y in Jan-Feb 2024; Cape +74 y/y
route_substitution_constraint: Cape of Good Hope is the main substitute for Asia-Europe/Red Sea avoidance. It preserves cargo movement but uses more vessel-days and fuel and can create knock-on port and inventory delays.
spare_capacity_or_rerouting: IMF says diversions around the Cape increased delivery times by 10 days or more on average; World Bank says detours inflated journey distances for cargo and tankers by up to 53%.
price_or_cost_channel: World Bank reports freight rates rose and shipping insurance costs reached unprecedented heights; this slice does not include a public war-risk premium series.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. End date and duration are blank because this row treats the disruption as ongoing/not cleanly closed for comparison purposes. The row is useful as a modern route-substitution analogue but not as a historical oil-supply-loss case.

baseline_or_market_size: 19.87 mb/d total oil via Hormuz in 2025; global liquids consumption about 104.0 mb/d
disrupted_share_pct: about 19% of world liquid-fuels consumption exposed
route_substitution_constraint: Some Saudi and UAE liquids can use bypass routes but capacity/quality/port constraints mean the aggregate oil flow is not fully substitutable; Kuwait Qatar Iraq and Iran have much weaker bypass options
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA collective stock action and country SPR/commercial inventory bridges offset part of the shock; examples include U.S. IEA allocation 172.2 mb and Japan allocation 79.8 mb; China public evidence supports commercial stock draw but not clean SPR accounting
replacement_or_demand_response: Replacement comes from Atlantic Basin/Russia/non-Hormuz sourcing, refinery-run cuts, inventory draw, and demand destruction; country F6R rows quantify crude adjustment for China India Japan Korea Europe and the United States
price_or_cost_channel: Broad oil price channel plus refined-product cracks freight and war-risk premiums; L8M sector table maps 15/25/40% low/base/high energy-price shocks into U.S. business cost bands
caveats: Headline exposure row, not an additive sum of product rows; local files do not provide a clean low/base/high aggregate disruption range after de-duplicating crude products LPG and refined-product overlaps.

baseline_or_market_size: 14.95 mb/d crude and condensate via Hormuz in 2025; implied global crude trade denominator about 44 mb/d
disrupted_share_pct: about 34% of global crude trade exposed
route_substitution_constraint: Saudi East-West and UAE/Fujairah options reduce but do not eliminate exposure; Iraq Kuwait Qatar and Iran are highly route-constrained; substitute crude grades and sanctions risk limit rapid replacement
inventory_or_policy_buffer: Oil stock policy buffers are strongest for IEA members; China commercial/operational draw proxy is 25 mb already drawn and 0.5/1.0/1.8 mb/d low/base/high flow-equivalent but government SPR use is opaque
replacement_or_demand_response: F6R rows estimate crude replacement supply plus inventory draw plus demand destruction for China India Japan South Korea Europe and the United States; Japan and India have the clearest public replacement-procurement signals
price_or_cost_channel: Brent and delivered crude price shock; downstream refinery and freight channels propagate into products and energy-intensive sectors
caveats: Low is a sum of local country-component low scenarios for Saudi Iraq UAE Kuwait Qatar and Iran including mixed UAE/Kuwait crude-product rows; base uses the KMZ.7 product master estimate; high is full exposed crude/condensate benchmark.

baseline_or_market_size: 11.43 Bcf/d Qatar plus UAE 2025Q1 LNG run-rate; more than 110 bcm/year via Hormuz in 2025
disrupted_share_pct: almost one-fifth of global LNG trade and more than one-quarter of Asia LNG imports exposed
route_substitution_constraint: No practical maritime bypass for Qatar/UAE LNG; Oman or pipeline alternatives are not a scalable replacement for Qatari liquefaction exports
inventory_or_policy_buffer: Japan LNG stock about 21 days of national consumption; South Korea reserve rule 7 days mandatory plus 30 days preventive reserve; Europe storage helps global LNG balance indirectly but does not replace Asian cargoes
replacement_or_demand_response: Replacement supply is limited; F6R Asia aggregate estimates 0.02/0.05/0.08 bcm/d equivalent and most adjustment is demand rationing, fuel switching, cargo competition, and storage draw
price_or_cost_channel: Spot LNG and regional gas basis channel; power generation fertilizer industry and petrochemicals face rationing or higher gas costs
caveats: Scenario volumes sum Qatar and UAE local KMZ.3 rows in Bcf/d; master table reports the comparable base loss as about 0.30 bcm/d, so unit conversions should be handled before merging with historical gas cases.

baseline_or_market_size: 1.5 mb/d Gulf LPG exports in 2025; 30% of seaborne LPG exports crossed Hormuz in 2025
route_substitution_constraint: No true bypass market; non-Gulf LPG exists but tanker availability feedstock logistics contract positions and import-terminal limits constrain rapid substitution
inventory_or_policy_buffer: India has no public national LPG stock-duration series; public evidence supports allocation controls domestic yield maximization alternative suppliers and booking restrictions rather than a defensible days-of-cover number
replacement_or_demand_response: India LPG row estimates replacement 0.10/0.18/0.28 mb/d, inventory draw 0.03/0.06/0.10 mb/d, and demand destruction 0.05/0.08/0.12 mb/d against about 0.43 mb/d disrupted imports
price_or_cost_channel: Household cooking fuel petrochemical C3/C4 feedstock and shipping-cost channel; price effects spill into chemicals and consumer fuel subsidies
caveats: Local product master has a base disrupted volume but not a complete low/high range. Avoid double-counting with petroleum liquids totals and Qatar refined-products rows that include LPG/naphtha.

baseline_or_market_size: 3.3 mb/d Gulf refined-product exports in 2025
disrupted_share_pct: IEA/World Bank framing around roughly one-fifth of refined-product trade via Hormuz; local denominator still pending
route_substitution_constraint: Some Saudi and UAE product barrels can be rerouted through Red Sea or Fujairah/Oman options but Gulf product routes remain exposed; replacement runs outside the Gulf are constrained by crude/feedstock availability and product specifications
inventory_or_policy_buffer: Oil-product inventories and IEA stock releases can buffer some crude/product balances but public product-specific days of cover are weaker than crude stock data
replacement_or_demand_response: F6R Europe row captures crude/refined-product replacement 0.35/0.60/0.80 mb/d, inventory draw 0.10/0.20/0.35 mb/d, and demand destruction 0.02/0.08/0.18 mb/d; broader product substitution remains incompletely observed
price_or_cost_channel: Diesel jet fuel gasoline and middle-distillate cracks; transportation and warehousing cost pass-through is likely faster than broad services pass-through
caveats: Base uses KMZ.7 master table. High uses full 3.3 mb/d exposed benchmark. KMZ.3 country product rows sum above this because they include route proxies and overlapping LPG/naphtha/mixed oil baskets.

baseline_or_market_size: About half of global seaborne sulphur trade moves through Hormuz; local Qatar UAE Saudi component baseline 8.623 Mt/year
disrupted_share_pct: about 50% of global seaborne sulphur trade exposed
route_substitution_constraint: Limited near-term substitute origin flexibility; Canada Kazakhstan and other non-Gulf sources can help but sour-gas/oil processing terminal stocks and phosphate-chain operating rates constrain replacement
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No reliable public inventory series or strategic sulphur reserve; S49 flags this as high-confidence exposure but low-confidence buffer information
replacement_or_demand_response: F6R sulfur row characterizes replacement low/base/high as medium_low/medium/medium_high rather than tonnage; affected chains are China and Morocco phosphate producers plus broader acid/fertilizer users
price_or_cost_channel: Sulphuric acid phosphate fertilizer refining and critical-mineral chemistry feedstock channel; price pressure transmits to MAP/DAP and crop-input costs
caveats: Low/base/high are summed local Qatar UAE Saudi country-component scenarios, not observed cargo losses; sulphur is upstream of phosphate fertilizer, so do not add mechanically to fertilizer output rows.

baseline_or_market_size: Upstream Hormuz countries account for 34% of global urea trade 23% of ammonia trade and 18% of MAP/DAP trade; local urea plus ammonia component baseline 16.839 Mt/year
disrupted_share_pct: 34% of global urea trade; 23% of ammonia trade; 18% of MAP/DAP trade exposed
route_substitution_constraint: Some cargo rerouting and land corridors exist but nitrogen fertilizer depends on Gulf gas/ammonia capacity and phosphate fertilizers also depend on sulphur and ammonia feedstocks; planting seasons make delays costly
inventory_or_policy_buffer: FAO/IFA evidence says no coordinated strategic fertilizer reserve; India has seasonal stock proxies such as about 41 days of urea and 67 days of DAP based on March 2026 parliamentary data
replacement_or_demand_response: F6R fertilizer row rates replacement low/base/high as medium_low/medium/medium_high; demand response can mean delayed purchases or lower application rather than immediate consumption destruction
price_or_cost_channel: Food-cost channel via nitrogen and phosphate fertilizers; downstream agriculture and food manufacturing exposure appears in L8M Food tier-2 input/freight band
caveats: Scenario volumes sum local Saudi/Qatar/Bahrain urea and ammonia component rows and therefore mix product and feedstock flows. Use trade-share columns for normalized historical comparison; do not add ammonia and urea as independent final losses without feedstock accounting.

baseline_or_market_size: About 5 Mt/year shipped through Hormuz; Gulf producers about 8% of global aluminium supply; local Bahrain component baseline 0.968 Mt/year
disrupted_share_pct: about 8% of global aluminium supply exposed
route_substitution_constraint: Route alternatives are not yet reconciled for all Gulf smelters; Bahrain is a clean Hormuz-dependent flow while UAE/Qatar/Saudi route shares need stronger confirmation
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No strategic aluminium stockpile row in local buffer table; production offset is slower and depends on power alumina smelter capacity and downstream demand
replacement_or_demand_response: F6R aluminium row rates replacement low/base/high as medium_low/medium/medium_high rather than tonnage; likely adjustment is delayed shipments, substitution from non-Gulf metal, and demand rationing
price_or_cost_channel: Primary metals input-cost channel; L8M primary metals row estimates U.S. direct energy added-cost sensitivity of 0.866/2.475/5.940 billion dollars under low/base/high shocks
caveats: Exposed flow uses the Gulf-wide IEA/KMZ master benchmark. Low/base/high tonnages are only the Bahrain local KMZ.3 component because full Gulf aluminium scenario volumes are not resolved.

baseline_or_market_size: 0.10-0.15% hull value pre-war additional war-risk premium benchmark; broader baseline is all Hormuz-linked vessel traffic
disrupted_share_pct: war-risk premium roughly 8x-30x normal in cited periods; applies across affected cargoes
route_substitution_constraint: Not a physical substitute-flow row; route avoidance increases voyage days and fleet requirements while insurance availability and charter willingness can bind even when cargo exists
inventory_or_policy_buffer: Strategic commodity stocks reduce urgency for some cargoes but do not directly offset vessel-day losses or insurance premia
replacement_or_demand_response: Demand response is indirect: cargo deferral, slower voyages, route changes, smaller spot participation, and higher delivered-cost pass-through
price_or_cost_channel: War-risk premiums freight demurrage security escorts and rerouting costs multiply oil LNG LPG fertilizer metals and container/bulk channels
caveats: Low/base/high are insurance-cost scenarios from the KMZ.7 freight/war-risk row, not disrupted physical flow.
